The backward bending supply curve of labor occurs when, at higher wage rates, the income effect dominates the substitution effect. As wages rise, workers may choose to work less and enjoy more leisure, even though the substitution effect would encourage more work. This leads to a decrease in quantity of labor supplied after a certain wage level — hence “backward bending.”

- Option 2 is incorrect — it only mentions substitution effect, which drives forward bending.
